name: QSBR — Quiescent-State-Based Reclamation
summary: Variant of EBR/RCU where threads explicitly announce quiescent points (no live references held) instead of bracketing critical sections. Among the lowest-overhead read-side schemes when the program already has natural quiescent points; ordinary reads need no per-access synchronisation. Production: liburcu-qsbr, DPDK rte_rcu_qsbr, Linux user-mode QSBR.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
QSBR is a variant of EBR/RCU where threads explicitly announce *quiescent points* — moments at which they hold no live references to shared data — instead of bracketing each critical section. Among the lowest-overhead read-side schemes when the program already has natural quiescent points: ordinary reads need no per-access synchronisation, while one quiescent announcement per outer loop iteration amortises barrier cost across many reads.

DPDK's per-data-structure quiescent-state variables let writers wait only on the readers actually relevant to a given structure, avoiding the global-grace-period pessimism of classical kernel RCU.

sections.title: Cost model and trade-offs
sections.content:
Cost: reader zero per access; one relaxed store per quiescent point. Trade-off: requires cooperative threads that periodically reach a "no references held" state — incompatible with arbitrary library code that may hold references across operation boundaries.

The canonical fit is packet-processing main loops (DPDK), kernel scheduler ticks (Linux), and request/response servers where each request boundary is a natural QS. The misfit is libraries that hand out references whose lifetime extends across QS announcements — those need HP/EBR-style per-access protection instead.

Production: **liburcu-qsbr**, **DPDK `rte_rcu_qsbr`** (packet-loop quiescence model), Linux user-mode QSBR.
